Capstone Project – Phase A (61998)

**OmniSight**

Distributed Semantic Video Intelligence

& Predictive Reasoning Platform

**Ahmad Tawil**

**Cyrine Fahoum**

Supervisor: Dr. Reuven Cohen

Contents

[Abstract	4](#abstract)

[**Modern video infrastructure produces footage faster than humans can review it. Surveillance, sports, and traffic cameras run continuously, yet the moments that matter remain buried in archives searched by timestamp rather than by meaning — the dark-data problem. Existing systems address narrow slices of it (open-vocabulary retrieval, anomaly detection, event anticipation, post-hoc summarisation), but rarely combine them end-to-end, and almost never measure how a shared representation transfers across visual domains.	4**](#heading=)

[**OmniSight is a distributed platform that brings these capabilities into a single pipeline. It ingests live and uploaded video, indexes each segment through dual semantic (SigLIP 2\) and temporal (InternVideo2) embeddings into Qdrant, and serves natural-language search at sub-second latency. A Predictive Forecasting Module (PFM) — a shared temporal-transformer backbone with three lightweight domain heads (surveillance, sports, traffic) trained jointly — predicts event type, time-to-event, and confidence over a sliding window. A four-tier agentic cascade then escalates only high-confidence candidates to a generative VLM, and after operator confirmation a multimodal RAG engine assembles an evidence-grounded, multi-camera dossier from the same vector store. All capabilities are exposed through both a web dashboard and an MCP server.	4**](#heading=)

[**The novel contribution is the PFM and its cross-domain transfer protocol: the hypothesis that pre-training the shared backbone on two domains lifts accuracy on a held-out third over training from scratch. The system will be evaluated on VIRAT, UCF-Crime \[8\], SoccerNet \[16\], DoTA \[7\], and the AI City Challenge \[18\] against six acceptance criteria. Phase A delivers the complete design, six formal algorithms, and the empirical protocol; Phase B will deliver the implementation and results	4**](#heading=)

[3.5.2 Algorithm 2 – Dual-Stream Embedding Generation	18](#3.5.2-algorithm-2-–-dual-stream-embedding-generation)

[3.5.4 Predictive Forecasting Module (PFM)	19](#3.5.4-predictive-forecasting-module-\(pfm\))

[Hypothesis. Pre-training the shared backbone on two domains produces transferable temporal representations that lift accuracy on a held-out third domain over training from scratch on it alone.	20](#hypothesis.-pre-training-the-shared-backbone-on-two-domains-produces-transferable-temporal-representations-that-lift-accuracy-on-a-held-out-third-domain-over-training-from-scratch-on-it-alone.)

[Protocol. Train backbone \+ heads jointly on {d1, d2}; freeze backbone and discard the d1, d2 heads; initialize a fresh head for d3 and fine-tune only that head on a k-shot subset of d3 (e.g., 10%); measure Top-3 accuracy on d3 test set; compare against a from-scratch baseline trained on the same k-shot subset. The protocol is run for all three holdout combinations. Acceptance criterion: \*\*SM-5\*\* — ≥10% Top-3 accuracy lift averaged across holdouts. A negative result is reported as a genuine empirical finding on the limits of cross-domain temporal transfer.	20](#protocol.-train-backbone-+-heads-jointly-on-{d1,-d2};-freeze-backbone-and-discard-the-d1,-d2-heads;-initialize-a-fresh-head-for-d3-and-fine-tune-only-that-head-on-a-k-shot-subset-of-d3-\(e.g.,-10%\);-measure-top-3-accuracy-on-d3-test-set;-compare-against-a-from-scratch-baseline-trained-on-the-same-k-shot-subset.-the-protocol-is-run-for-all-three-holdout-combinations.-acceptance-criterion:-**sm-5**-—-≥10%-top-3-accuracy-lift-averaged-across-holdouts.-a-negative-result-is-reported-as-a-genuine-empirical-finding-on-the-limits-of-cross-domain-temporal-transfer.)

# 

# 

# 

# **Abstract** {#abstract}

# Modern video infrastructure produces footage faster than humans can review it. Surveillance, sports, and traffic cameras run continuously, yet the moments that matter remain buried in archives searched by timestamp rather than by meaning — the dark-data problem. Existing systems address narrow slices of it (open-vocabulary retrieval, anomaly detection, event anticipation, post-hoc summarisation), but rarely combine them end-to-end, and almost never measure how a shared representation transfers across visual domains.

# OmniSight is a distributed platform that brings these capabilities into a single pipeline. It ingests live and uploaded video, indexes each segment through dual semantic (SigLIP 2\) and temporal (InternVideo2) embeddings into Qdrant, and serves natural-language search at sub-second latency. A Predictive Forecasting Module (PFM) — a shared temporal-transformer backbone with three lightweight domain heads (surveillance, sports, traffic) trained jointly — predicts event type, time-to-event, and confidence over a sliding window. A four-tier agentic cascade then escalates only high-confidence candidates to a generative VLM, and after operator confirmation a multimodal RAG engine assembles an evidence-grounded, multi-camera dossier from the same vector store. All capabilities are exposed through both a web dashboard and an MCP server.

# The novel contribution is the PFM and its cross-domain transfer protocol: the hypothesis that pre-training the shared backbone on two domains lifts accuracy on a held-out third over training from scratch. The system will be evaluated on VIRAT, UCF-Crime \[8\], SoccerNet \[16\], DoTA \[7\], and the AI City Challenge \[18\] against six acceptance criteria. Phase A delivers the complete design, six formal algorithms, and the empirical protocol; Phase B will deliver the implementation and results

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# **1\. Introduction**

Video is now one of the most abundant data sources in the modern world. Surveillance networks, sports broadcasts, and traffic cameras record continuously, yet finding a specific moment inside this footage – such as a package left at a doorway, or the seconds leading up to a collision – often requires hours of watching recordings. As video archives grow faster than humans can review them, much of the captured data remains inaccessible. In practice, long-form videos are difficult to index, search, and analyze quickly enough to support operational decisions, some incidents are missed, insights arrive late, and investigators still spend hours reconstructing timelines manually. Video is recorded everywhere, yet practically usable nowhere – the "dark data" problem at the heart of this project. 

This challenge has drawn significant attention from both research and industry. A rich ecosystem of work has emerged around video understanding – spanning object recognition, natural-language retrieval, behavioral anomaly detection, predictive analytics, and reasoning with vision-language models – and commercial platforms increasingly combine several of these capabilities into unified products. The field is evolving rapidly, with new architectures and deployment patterns appearing continuously.

OmniSight is designed as a distributed platform for semantic video intelligence that brings these capabilities together in a single end-to-end pipeline. The system ingests both live streams and uploaded video, indexes their content by meaning rather than by file or timestamp, and lets users search this content using everyday language. Beyond searching what has already happened, OmniSight also looks ahead, a Predictive Forecasting Module recognizes early signs of an unfolding event and raises a warning before the event completes. Each warning is then double-checked by a short verification cascade so that obvious false alarms are filtered out before reaching the user. When an incident does occur, the system assembles the relevant footage from one or more cameras into a structured summary that an investigator can read in minutes instead of hours. The same capabilities are offered to people through a web dashboard and to other AI agents through a standard interface (the Model Context Protocol). Concretely, the project pursues five goals: enable natural-language semantic search over video archives across surveillance, sports, and traffic, forecast event type, time-to-event, and confidence with measurable early-warning time across all three domains using a shared temporal model, reduce false alarms through a layered verification cascade, produce evidence-grounded multi-camera incident summaries, and make all core capabilities accessible to external AI agents through a standard protocol. 

The remainder of this document is organized as follows: Chapter 2 reviews related literature, Chapter 3 presents the engineering process, Chapter 4 defines the evaluation metrics and test plan, and Chapter 5 documents the AI tools used during Phase A.

# **2\. Background and Literature Review** {#2.-background-and-literature-review}

## **2.1 Background** {#2.1-background}

Understanding the content of continuous video at scale draws on several lines of research that have, until recently, evolved largely in parallel. Contrastive vision-language pretraining produced shared image-text embedding spaces that enabled open-vocabulary search over still frames (Radford et al., 2021; Tschannen et al., 2025), while temporal video foundation models extended these representations to capture motion and event structure (Wang et al., 2024; Qwen Team, 2025). In parallel, the surveillance, sports, and traffic communities developed dedicated methods for detecting and anticipating rare events in long untrimmed footage (Sultani et al., 2018; Felsen et al., 2017; Giancola et al., 2018; Oh et al., 2011; Yao et al., 2022; Cao et al., 2023), and the database community contributed approximate nearest-neighbour indexes that make sub-second retrieval over billions of vectors tractable (Malkov & Yashunin, 2018). More recently, retrieval-augmented generation has begun to ground vision–language reasoning in stored evidence, supporting structured summarisation without task-specific fine-tuning (Mao et al., 2025; Loumachi et al., 2025). Each of these strands has a strong foundation in isolation, yet the literature offers limited evidence on how they behave when combined into a single semantic pipeline across heterogeneous video domains. 

## **2.2 Literature Review** {#2.2-literature-review}

This section reviews the research areas underpinning modern semantic video understanding, open-vocabulary vision–language search, temporal video understanding, event anticipation, anomaly detection with causal reasoning, and retrieval-augmented generation. Each area is summarised with its main limitations, followed by a representative model.

### **2.2.1 Vision-Language Models for Open-Vocabulary Search** {#2.2.1-vision-language-models-for-open-vocabulary-search}

Open-vocabulary visual search retrieves visual content using free-form natural-language descriptions rather than predefined categories. The dominant approach learns a shared image–text embedding space contrastively – matching pairs are pulled together while mismatched pairs are pushed apart – so similarity reduces to an inner product. CLIP (Radford et al., 2021\) established this paradigm by training dual encoders on \~400M web image–text pairs, enabling zero-shot transfer across domains. **Two limitations** persist. First, these models operate on individual frames and cannot represent motion or temporal context – distinctions such as “opening” versus “closing a door” are lost. Second, training on short captions biases them toward salient objects, leaving fine-grained spatial reasoning and counting weak. These gaps motivate the temporal models in §2.2.2 and the reasoning VLMs in §2.2.4.

#### **2.2.1.1 Contrastive Vision–Language Embedding (SigLIP 2\)**

SigLIP 2 (Tschannen et al., 2025\) is among the strongest publicly available open-vocabulary models. It replaces CLIP’s batch-wide softmax with a sigmoid loss that scores each image-text pair independently, improving training stability at large batch sizes. SigLIP 2 adds captioning-based pretraining (a decoder generates captions and bounding-box references from image features), self-supervised dense-feature objectives, and refined data curation, producing tighter visual-textual alignment than earlier CLIP-style models. The architecture is a standard dual encoder, a **Vision Transformer (ViT)** – a model that splits an image into fixed-size patches and processes them with self-attention, the mechanism by which each patch attends to every other patch – encodes the image, while a Transformer text encoder maps the caption into the same space. Matches are scored by cosine similarity. SigLIP 2 reports consistent gains on zero-shot classification, image–text retrieval, transfer, open-vocabulary localisation, and dense prediction.

![][image1]

  **Figure 1: SigLIP 2 Architecture (Source: Tschannen et al., 2025 )**

### **2.2.2 Temporal Video Understanding** {#2.2.2-temporal-video-understanding}

Temporal video understanding represents motion and event structure that emerge only across multiple frames. The literature has progressed through three architectural generations: two-stream CNNs (Convolutional Neural Networks that process appearance and motion in parallel pathways – RGB frames and optical flow, the per-pixel motion field between frames). 3D convolutional networks, which extend convolutions into the time dimension to learn spatio-temporal filters from stacked frames; and video transformers, which apply self-attention over the spatio-temporal volume, treating video patches as tokens. Pretraining has shifted from supervised action classification toward masked video modelling and video-text contrastive learning, yielding general-purpose video foundation models. **Two limitations** stand out. First, most models are trained on short clips of a few seconds, extending effective temporal context to minutes or hours remains open and matters for long-form retrieval. Second, the dominant objectives target retrospective understanding rather than forecasting – anticipation (§2.2.3) is treated as a separate task.

#### **2.2.2.1 Representative Model: InternVideo2** 

InternVideo2 (Wang et al., 2024\) is a widely cited video foundation model trained with a progressive scheme combining three objectives: masked video modelling, in which parts of the video are hidden and the model reconstructs them; multimodal contrastive learning across video, text, and audio, which aligns matching triplets in a shared embedding space; and next-token prediction over interleaved video–language sequences. Together these capture appearance, motion, semantics, and discourse-level structure in one backbone. The model encodes short clips through stacked spatio-temporal attention blocks – self-attention applied jointly across the spatial dimensions of each frame and across frames – producing clip-level embeddings that represent entire actions rather than isolated images. The authors report state-of-the-art or competitive results on over 60 video benchmarks spanning action recognition, retrieval, video question answering, and temporal localisation, making it a natural choice when motion sensitivity is required.

![][image2]

**Figure 2: InternVideo2 Architecture (Source: Wang et al., 2024\)**

### **2.2.3 Event Anticipation and Temporal Transformers** {#2.2.3-event-anticipation-and-temporal-transformers}

Event anticipation predicts what is about to happen from a partially observed video – relevant for driver assistance, proactive surveillance, and sports analytics. Modern anticipation models build on the Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017), in which self-attention is extended along the temporal axis to capture long-range dependencies across frame or clip embeddings. Three main variants exist: encoder–decoder anticipation transformers that emit future action labels; predictive auto-encoders that reconstruct unobserved future frames and use reconstruction error as a signal; and trajectory transformers that forecast the motion of detected agents. Cao et al. (2023) introduced the **NWPU Campus** dataset – the first large-scale benchmark for video anomaly anticipation – together with a forward–backward scene-conditioned auto-encoder that predicts future frames from past context and uses the reconstruction error as an early-warning signal. Felsen et al. (2017) showed that trajectory-based forecasting can predict player actions in basketball, generalising anticipation from raw pixels to symbolic motion over agents. Yao et al. (2022) extended unsupervised anticipation to driving with the DoTA dataset for traffic-anomaly detection. **The main limitation** is that anticipation benchmarks are typically single-domain, and shared representations that generalise across very different visual regimes (campus, sports court, road) remain under-explored.

### **2.2.4 Anomaly Detection and Causal Reasoning** {#2.2.4-anomaly-detection-and-causal-reasoning}

Video anomaly detection identifies segments that deviate from normal behaviour. Because anomalies are rare and diverse, the field relies on *weakly supervised* formulations – where only coarse video-level labels indicate whether *some* anomaly occurs without localising it – and *self-supervised* formulations, where the model learns a notion of normality from unlabelled footage and flags departures from it. UCF-Crime (Sultani et al., 2018), containing roughly 1,900 CCTV videos, remains the standard benchmark for detection from video-level labels. A recent shift moves beyond binary detection toward *causal reasoning*. VADER (Cheng et al., 2026\) exemplifies this direction, combining object-relation features – structured representations of which objects appear and how they interact – with a vision-language model to generate textual explanations of *why* an event is abnormal, not only *that* it is. **The significant limitation** of such systems is that the reasoning is *post hoc*: it is produced only after an anomaly has already been detected. Integrating causal reasoning directly into the anticipatory phase, so that explanations accompany rather than follow a prediction, remains an open challenge.

#### **2.2.4.1 Representative Model: Qwen2.5-VL**

Qwen2.5-VL (Bai et al., 2025\) is a large pretrained vision–language model that accepts images or short clips with natural-language prompts and produces grounded, free-form text. Unlike contrastive encoders such as CLIP and SigLIP 2, which emit fixed embedding vectors for matching and retrieval, Qwen2.5-VL is generative: it describes scenes, answers questions, follows instructions, performs spatial grounding (locating objects or regions referenced in the prompt), and produces multi-step reasoning chains. Architecturally, the model combines a ViT encoder with a large language model (LLM) decoder – a Transformer trained to generate text one token at a time conditioned on prior context – linked through a learned MLP projection that maps visual features into the language model’s input space. The vision encoder uses dynamic resolution processing and window attention (self-attention restricted to local windows to reduce cost) to handle native-resolution input efficiently, while a multimodal rotary position embedding aligned to absolute time enables second-level event localisation in videos lasting up to hours. The model is released openly in 3B, 7B, and 72B sizes and is among the strongest open VLMs on visual question answering, document understanding, and video benchmarks – making it a representative example of generative VLMs that serve as reasoning engines on top of detection or retrieval components.

![][image3]

### **2.2.5 Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Forensic Reconstruction** {#2.2.5-retrieval-augmented-generation-for-forensic-reconstruction}

Post-incident investigation traditionally requires manual timeline reconstruction from heterogeneous evidence. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) automates parts of this workflow by conditioning a generative model on evidence retrieved from an external store via dense vector similarity search. GenDFIR (Loumachi et al., 2025\) applies RAG to cyber-incident timelines, producing evidence-grounded reports, Multi-RAG (Mao et al., 2025\) extends RAG across video, audio, and text for multimodal video understanding. **Two limitations** remain. First, most forensic RAG systems are text-only and do not retrieve from a semantic video representation, limiting use when evidence is primarily visual. Second, multimodal RAG systems consuming video target general understanding (captioning, summarisation, open-domain QA) rather than structured forensic reconstruction, where outputs must be evidence-traceable and follow a domain-specific narrative.

#### ***2.2.5.1 Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)***

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (Lewis et al., 2020\) conditions a generative model on evidence retrieved on the fly from an external knowledge store. Retrieval is typically approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search in a dense vector space using indexes such as HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World; Malkov & Yashunin, 2018), a graph-based index that achieves sub-second retrieval over billions of vectors. Grounding in retrieved content reduces hallucination, improves factual accuracy, and makes outputs traceable to sources. Multimodal RAG generalises this beyond text, retrieving images, clips, audio, or structured records alongside text and passing them to a VLM or multimodal LLM. This is particularly relevant when evidence is heterogeneous (e.g., surveillance footage interleaved with sensor logs) and the desired output is a structured, evidence-grounded narrative.

**![][image4]**

### **2.2.6 Research Gap and Contribution** {#2.2.6-research-gap-and-contribution}

Prior work has advanced each of the above areas in isolation: open-vocabulary retrieval (CLIP, SigLIP 2), temporal video understanding (InternVideo2), event anticipation (NWPU Campus, Felsen et al., DoTA), causal anomaly reasoning (VADER), and multimodal or forensic RAG (GenDFIR, Multi-RAG). In parallel, AI-driven video platforms have become increasingly integrated and, in some cases, proactive. What remains less established in the open literature is a single, reproducible architecture that jointly studies: (i) multi-domain event anticipation through a shared semantic backbone; (ii) causal explanation of predictions tightly coupled to the anticipatory signal; and (iii) automated multimodal forensic reconstruction grounded in the same vector store — evaluated across heterogeneous domains such as surveillance, sports, and traffic. Bridging these capabilities into one end-to-end pipeline, and measuring cross-domain transfer, is an open research direction that OmniSight addresses.

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# **3\. Engineering Process** {#3.-engineering-process}

## **3.1 Phase A Progress** {#3.1-phase-a-progress}

Phase A followed a *research-then-design* workflow, moving from problem framing to a buildable specification through a sequence of deliberate engineering decisions. The five activity tracks below summarise how the gap identified in Chapter 2 was translated into the architecture, datasets, and algorithms that Phase B will implement and evaluate.

**Literature survey** — Reviewed five areas — open-vocabulary retrieval, temporal video understanding, event anticipation, anomaly detection, and retrieval-augmented generation — and crystallised the PFM cross-domain-transfer hypothesis as the scientific contribution.

**Requirements (§3.2)** — Specified the functional and non-functional requirements the platform must satisfy.

**Architecture (§3.3)** — Translated requirements into a containerised microservice deployment, an asynchronous ingestion-to-inference pipeline, and an MCP-based interface for external agents.

**Datasets (§3.4)** — Selected five public datasets — VIRAT, UCF-Crime, SoccerNet, DoTA, and the AI City Challenge — covering the three target domains and aligned to the acceptance criteria.

**Algorithms (§3.5)** — Designed six core algorithms with pseudocode in Appendix A, paying particular attention to the contracts between stages.

## **Phase A deliverables**

* This project book.

* System architecture and deployment diagrams.

* Six formal algorithms with pseudocode (Appendix A).

* Dataset selection and acceptance criteria.

* Test plan (§4.2).

* Phase B implementation backlog implied by the requirements.

**Phase B** will execute this backlog and produce empirical results against the acceptance

## **3.2 Requirements**

### 3.2.1 Functional Requirements (FR) {#3.2.1-functional-requirements-(fr)}

The following table enumerates the core functional requirements of the OmniSight platform, spanning video ingestion, AI processing, semantic search, predictive forecasting, agentic verification, forensic reporting, enterprise interoperability, and fault tolerance.

| ID | Functional Requirement |
| :---- | :---- |
| **FR-1a** | The system shall ingest video from RTSP streams and uploaded video files. |
| **FR-1b** | The system shall select informative frames for downstream processing based on detected motion activity. |
| **FR-1c** | The system shall publish selected frames to an asynchronous processing queue for decoupled worker consumption. |
| **FR-2** | The system shall generate frame-level semantic embeddings and clip-level temporal embeddings for each processed video segment. |
| **FR-3** | The system shall store embeddings together with metadata (timestamp, camera identifier, thumbnail reference) in a vector database. |
| **FR-4** | The system shall accept natural-language queries, encode them into the shared embedding space, and return ranked results combining frame-level and temporal similarity scores. |
| **FR-5** | The Predictive Forecasting Module shall consume temporal embedding sequences over configurable sliding windows (30–60 seconds) and output event-type probabilities, time-to-event estimates, and confidence scores. |
| **FR-6** | The system shall verify candidate alerts through a multi-stage pipeline that combines similarity retrieval, predictive scoring, causal reasoning, and operator escalation before issuing alerts to human operators. |
| **FR-7** | The system shall generate structured forensic dossiers from retrieved vector-database evidence using a multimodal RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) engine. |
| **FR-8** | The system shall expose semantic search, temporal analysis, predictive alerts, and live alert streaming as an MCP-compliant server for external AI agents. |
| **FR-9** | The system shall provide a web dashboard for natural-language search, real-time alert display, and forensic dossier viewing. |
| **FR-10** | The system shall enforce authentication on all API, MCP, and dashboard endpoints. |
| **FR-11** | The system shall automatically retry failed processing operations until success or explicit failure classification. |

### 3.2.2 Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) {#3.2.2-non-functional-requirements-(nfr)}

Non-functional requirements are organized according to the ISO 25010 software quality model.

| ID | Quality Attribute | Requirement | Target |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **NFR-1** | Performance Efficiency | End-to-end indexing latency from frame capture to searchable entry | \< 5 seconds |
| **NFR-2** | Performance Efficiency | Semantic search query response time | \< 3 seconds |
| **NFR-3** | Performance Efficiency | PFM predictive inference time per sliding window | \< 2 seconds |
| **NFR-4** | Performance Efficiency | Concurrent video streams supported without processing degradation | ≥ 10 streams |
| **NFR-5** | Reliability | Data loss during component failures | 0 embeddings lost |
| **NFR-6** | Security | Authentication and role-based access control enforcement | 100% of endpoints |
| **NFR-7a** | Maintainability | Services shall be independently deployable | All services containerized |
| **NFR-7b** | Maintainability | New PFM domain adapter heads shall be addable without modifying the shared backbone or other system components | Verified by extension |
| **NFR-8** | Portability | The full system shall be deployable as containerized services on any Docker-compatible host via a single orchestration command | Single command deployment |

## **3.3 System Architecture** {#3.3-system-architecture}

### **3.3.1 Use Case** {#3.3.1-use-case}

The diagram shows three actors and the capabilities each one exercises. **Users** (operators and analysts) upload video, run semantic search, view the dashboard, receive predictive alerts, and generate forensic dossiers. **System Administrators** manage users and roles, configure cameras, monitor system health, and set the retention policy. The **External AI Agent** consumes OmniSight's capabilities through the MCP interface — semantic search, temporal action analysis, the live alert stream, and predictive alert retrieval.  
*Include* relationships make **Authenticate** mandatory for user and admin actions, and **Invoke MCP Tool** mandatory for every external-agent action. The *extend* relationship between **Generate Forensic Dossier** and **Confirm or Dismiss Alert** captures an optional flow: confirming an alert may trigger dossier generation.

![][image5]

### **3.3.2 High-level Deployment Architecture** {#3.3.2-high-level-deployment-architecture}

OmniSight is deployed as **containerized microservices** orchestrated by **Docker Compose** (a tool that defines and runs multi-container applications from a single configuration). Every component runs in its own container on a shared internal network, keeping the system portable and letting individual services scale or restart independently.

The deployment is organized into three tiers. The **Frontend Tier** serves the React Dashboard through an Nginx container, the entry point for human operators over HTTPS. The **Backend Tier** contains four microservices: the Ingestion Engine (frame extraction via OpenCV/FFmpeg), the AI Workers (SigLIP 2 and InternVideo2 on GPU, horizontally scalable), the PFM \+ Verification service (temporal transformer plus VLM reasoning), and the Query Service (FastAPI for REST, FastMCP for MCP). The **Data Tier** holds Qdrant (vector database for embeddings) and PostgreSQL (metadata and alert logs).

Between the backend services sits **RabbitMQ**, a message broker that decouples ingestion from inference. Frames are published to work queues and consumed asynchronously by the AI Workers, with a dead-letter queue catching failures for retry. Two external entry points exist: operators reach the dashboard over HTTPS, while external AI agents connect directly to the Query Service over MCP/JSON-RPC.

### **3.3.3 MCP Server Integration** {#3.3.3-mcp-server-integration}

OmniSight is exposed to external AI agents through a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, implemented with FastMCP. This lets any agent — for example a security, stadium, or traffic agent — invoke OmniSight's capabilities natively, without bespoke integration code.

Each agent runs inside a host application that instantiates a dedicated **MCP client** (a small protocol component that opens and maintains the connection on the agent's behalf). The client holds a stateful **JSON-RPC 2.0** session (a lightweight JSON-based request/response format) with the server over Streamable HTTP / SSE. The session opens with a lifecycle handshake (initialize and capability negotiation), during which the server advertises its **primitives** — the standard things an MCP server can expose: tools (callable functions) and resources (readable or streamable data).

OmniSight exposes four primitives: three tools — search\_video\_semantics, analyze\_temporal\_action, and get\_predictive\_alerts — and one streaming resource, live\_alert\_stream. Tool calls are routed internally to the OmniSight backend (Qdrant, PFM, and the Agentic Verification Layer), while verified alert events are pushed back to subscribed clients over the same persistent connection.  
This design satisfies functional requirement FR-8 and the *MCP Interoperability* success metric.

**![][image6]**

## **3.4 Datasets** {#3.4-datasets}

OmniSight will be trained and evaluated on five publicly available datasets covering its three target domains – surveillance, sports, and traffic. This spread is intentional, the Predictive Forecasting Module uses a shared temporal backbone with per-domain adapter heads, so evaluation requires datasets that differ in viewpoint, event type, and visual regime, yet each carry annotations rich enough for training and measurement.

**3.4.1 VIRAT (Oh et al., 2011\)** \- Surveillance. Stationary outdoor-camera footage with object tracks and event annotations, primary dataset for ingestion, frame selection, embedding generation, and semantic-search evaluation (FR-1, FR-2, FR-4, SM-1).

**3.4.2 UCF-Crime (Sultani et al., 2018\) \-** Surveillance. 1,900 untrimmed surveillance videos across 13 anomaly classes. will be used to train the PFM surveillance adapter and to evaluate agentic verification and false-positive reduction (FR-5, FR-6, SM-2, SM-4). Precursor windows will be derived semi-automatically, as the standard release provides video-level labels.

**3.4.3 SoccerNet (Giancola et al., 2018\)** \- Sports. Broadcast soccer matches with dense timestamped events (goals, fouls, cards). will train the sports adapter and support cross-domain transfer evaluation (FR-5, SM-2, SM-5).

**3.4.4 DoTA (Yao et al., 2022\)** \- Traffic. 4,677 ego-centric dash-cam videos with anomaly categories and explicit start/end times. Its precursor–anomaly structure supports time-to-event and early-warning measurement and cross-domain generalization (FR-5, SM-2, SM-3, SM-5).

**3.4.5 AI City Challenge (2025)** \- Traffic. Multi-camera traffic-safety footage capturing incidents from synchronized viewpoints. the only dataset with overlapping multi-camera coverage, intended to validate cross-camera correlation in RAG forensic-dossier synthesis (FR-7, SM-6). Together these datasets will allow OmniSight to be evaluated across all three target domains, with the cross-domain transfer central to the Predictive Forecasting Module measured on genuinely distinct visual regimes rather than a single homogeneous corpus.

## **3.5 Algorithms**  {#3.5-algorithms}

This section presents the six core algorithms underpinning OmniSight. Each entry provides a short motivation followed by high-level pseudocode.

### **3.5.1 Algorithm 1 – ROI-Aware Spatial-Motion Sampling**  {#3.5.1-algorithm-1-–-roi-aware-spatial-motion-sampling}

The ingestion service holds a per-camera rolling buffer of length 2Δ. After a 2Δ warm-up, the sampler partitions the grayscale difference between f\_t and f\_{t−Δ} into a G×G grid and admits f\_t if at least k cells exceed τ ∈ \[0, 255\]. On admission, the causal clip W \= \[t − 2Δ, t\] is read from the buffer and (f\_t, W) is forwarded downstream. The causal window removes lookahead latency for real-time alerts and ensures W is fully buffered.

### **3.5.2 Algorithm 2 – Dual-Stream Embedding Generation** {#3.5.2-algorithm-2-–-dual-stream-embedding-generation}

Each forwarded payload is processed by two asynchronous workers. The frame-level path runs SigLIP 2 on f\_t for static scene semantics. The clip-level path runs InternVideo2\_clip (768-D) on W subsampled to its required 8 frames at 224×224 for temporal semantics. Both embeddings are L2-normalized and upserted into Qdrant with shared metadata (including frame\_id) so frame-level and clip-level results can be joined during fusion.

### **3.5.3 Dual-Model Score Fusion for Semantic Retrieval** {#3.5.3-dual-model-score-fusion-for-semantic-retrieval}

Queries vary by modality: scenes ("red car near entrance") vs. actions ("person running away"). A classifier C(q) outputs softmax weights (w\_f, w\_c). The query is encoded once by each text encoder (each model only operates in its own joint space). Because raw cosine scores from SigLIP 2 and InternVideo2 live on incomparable scales, fusion uses **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)** over rank positions — scale-invariant and the standard for combining heterogeneous retrievers. The constant **k₀ \= 60** is the value recommended by Cormack et al. (2009).

### **3.5.4 Predictive Forecasting Module (PFM)**  {#3.5.4-predictive-forecasting-module-(pfm)}

The PFM is OmniSight's novel research contribution: a shared temporal backbone with three domain heads, jointly trained, with cross-domain transfer evaluated as the primary scientific claim.

#### ***3.5.4.1 Inference***

A shared temporal-transformer backbone consumes a sliding window of n InternVideo2 clip embeddings (n \= ⌊T\_window / T\_clip⌋, T\_window ∈ \[30, 60\] s). Causal windows from Algorithm 1 anchor each embedding at the trailing edge t, predicted Δt is measured forward from t. Three lightweight domain heads produce an event-type distribution p and time-to-event Δt. confidence κ is the normalized inverse entropy of p. A transformer backbone is chosen because anticipation requires long-range temporal dependencies, transformers parallelize across the window, and prior anticipation work (Felsen et al., 2017; Cao et al., 2023\) reports stronger attention-based results.  
![][image7]

#### ***3.5.4.2 Training***

Each head is trained on its own dataset: surveillance → UCF-Crime (30–60 s precursor windows before anomaly onset), sports → SoccerNet (timestamped events), traffic → DoTA (cleanest, ships with exact start times). Because no dataset provides explicit precursor labels, we derive them semi-automatically by anchoring backwards from each event timestamp, with manual spot-checks on a sample; sports is the most exposed and is documented as a known limitation in §3.6.

The backbone and heads are trained jointly with:  
\> L \= Σ\_d λ\_d · \[CE(p\_d, y\_d) \+ α · MSE(Δt\_d, τ\_d)\]

where d ∈ {surveillance, sports, traffic}; λ\_d is the per-domain task weight; CE is the cross-entropy between predicted distribution p\_d and label y\_d; α weights the time-to-event regression; MSE is between predicted Δt\_d and ground-truth τ\_d (seconds). The per-domain trigger threshold θ\_d is tuned on a held-out validation split.  
![][image8]

#### ***3.5.4.3 Cross-Domain Transfer Protocol***

### Hypothesis. Pre-training the shared backbone on two domains produces transferable temporal representations that lift accuracy on a held-out third domain over training from scratch on it alone. {#hypothesis.-pre-training-the-shared-backbone-on-two-domains-produces-transferable-temporal-representations-that-lift-accuracy-on-a-held-out-third-domain-over-training-from-scratch-on-it-alone.}

### Protocol. Train backbone \+ heads jointly on {d1, d2}; freeze backbone and discard the d1, d2 heads; initialize a fresh head for d3 and fine-tune only that head on a k-shot subset of d3 (e.g., 10%); measure Top-3 accuracy on d3 test set; compare against a from-scratch baseline trained on the same k-shot subset. The protocol is run for all three holdout combinations. Acceptance criterion: \*\*SM-5\*\* — ≥10% Top-3 accuracy lift averaged across holdouts. A negative result is reported as a genuine empirical finding on the limits of cross-domain temporal transfer. {#protocol.-train-backbone-+-heads-jointly-on-{d1,-d2};-freeze-backbone-and-discard-the-d1,-d2-heads;-initialize-a-fresh-head-for-d3-and-fine-tune-only-that-head-on-a-k-shot-subset-of-d3-(e.g.,-10%);-measure-top-3-accuracy-on-d3-test-set;-compare-against-a-from-scratch-baseline-trained-on-the-same-k-shot-subset.-the-protocol-is-run-for-all-three-holdout-combinations.-acceptance-criterion:-**sm-5**-—-≥10%-top-3-accuracy-lift-averaged-across-holdouts.-a-negative-result-is-reported-as-a-genuine-empirical-finding-on-the-limits-of-cross-domain-temporal-transfer.}

![][image9]

### **3.5.5 Four-Tier Agentic Verification Pipeline** {#3.5.5-four-tier-agentic-verification-pipeline}

Continuous embeddings are filtered by a four-tier cascade. Tier 1 is a cheap concept-vector trigger. Tier 2 runs the PFM (Algorithm 4\) and gates on max(p) and κ. Tier 3 invokes Qwen2.5-VL on 8 sampled frames with a structured JSON prompt for a Chain-of-Causation verdict. Tier 4 escalates only Tier-3-passed alerts to the operator. Causal windows from Algorithm 1 mean each tier sees only fully buffered evidence, eliminating lookahead latency in alert delivery.

![][image10]

### **3.5.6 Multimodal RAG Forensic Synthesis** {#3.5.6-multimodal-rag-forensic-synthesis}

After operator confirmation, the RAG engine reconstructs an evidence-grounded dossier. The query combines a CoC summary (text) with the triggering clip vector. Retrieval is scoped by Qdrant payload filter to a window \[t − R, t \+ R\] around the incident (R is the retrieval scope, distinct from the buffer Δ). Cross-camera correlation groups evidence into 2-second bins, retaining items from FOV-overlapping cameras. Each item is tagged with a citation ID; the LLM is instructed to cite every claim, mirroring the pattern from Multi-RAG and GenDFIR.

## **3.6 Expected Challenges** {#3.6-expected-challenges}

The list below summarizes the primary technical challenges anticipated during OmniSight's development, why each one is difficult for our project specifically, and the mitigation strategy we plan to apply.

**PFM prediction accuracy:** The PFM must recognize precursor patterns before an event occurs, when the signal is subtle. Small errors in precursor-window placement turn anticipation into detection after the fact. **Mitigation:** Curriculum learning on precursor windows, empirical tuning of window size, and ensembling temporal models for stability.

**Cross-domain generalization:** The same backbone must serve surveillance, sports, and traffic – but precursor dynamics differ sharply across UCF-Crime, SoccerNet, and DoTA, so transfer is not guaranteed. **Mitigation:** Pre-train on a multi-domain corpus, fine-tune a lightweight head per domain, and evaluate transfer on held-out domains.

**Real-time processing load:** Ingestion, dual embedders(SigLip,InternVideo), Qdrant insertion, and PFM inference must all run under a \<5s per-frame budget on bounded student-grade GPUs. **Mitigation:** Motion-based frame selection, async RabbitMQ queue with horizontally scalable workers, and GPU batching across all model calls.

**VLM reasoning latency:** The VLM is the slowest component, invoking it on every frame would break real-time performance. **Mitigation:** Trigger the VLM only after vector-similarity and PFM stages flag a candidate, cache embeddings, and use quantized/distilled models (Quantized models compress weights for speed, while distilled models shrink massive networks while retaining intelligence). Fall back to PFM-only alerts if needed.

**Dataset quality and precursor labeling:** No single dataset covers all three domains with precursor labels. We use **UCF-Crime** (surveillance), **SoccerNet** (sports), **DoTA** (traffic), and **VIRAT** (search), but each was labeled for a different task – none ships with the precursor windows the PFM needs, so we must derive them ourselves. **AI City Challenge** is the only multi-camera source, which limits the number of cross-camera forensic test cases.**Mitigation:** Combine the four datasets for domain coverage, derive precursor labels semi-automatically by anchoring backwards from existing event timestamps (cleanest in DoTA and UCF-Crime), and perform manual spot-checks on a sample. Thin domains (sports most exposed) are documented as known limitations.

## **4\. Tests and Metrics** {#4.-tests-and-metrics}

### **4.1 Success Metrics and Acceptance Criteria** {#4.1-success-metrics-and-acceptance-criteria}

Each design goal maps to a measurable criterion, tested on the dataset best suited to it (see §3.4).

* SM-1 – Semantic Search Quality: The correct video clip appears in the top 5 results for at least 85% of natural-language queries, with a clear lift on action queries over a frame-only baseline. How to measure: Run a labelled query set on VIRAT (clips with known matches), count how often the right clip lands in the top 5, and compare against the frame-only baseline.

* SM-2 – Prediction Accuracy: The real event is among the model's top 3 predictions in at least 70% of cases, per domain. How to measure: On held-out clips from UCF-Crime (surveillance), SoccerNet (sports), and DoTA (traffic), check whether the annotated event appears in the model's three most likely predictions.

* SM-3 – Early Warning Time: Warnings fire at least 15 seconds before the event begins – enough time for human reaction. How to measure: On DoTA, which provides exact event start times, average the gap between each correct warning and the annotated event start.

* SM-4 – False Alarm Rate: No more than 20% of warnings are false, per domain. How to measure: On UCF-Crime (including its normal footage), divide warnings that don't match an annotated event by total warnings raised.

* SM-5 – Cross-Domain Learning: Pre-training on two domains lifts third-domain accuracy by at least 10% over training on that domain alone. How to measure: Train the backbone on two of {UCF-Crime, SoccerNet, DoTA}, fine-tune on the third, and compare to a from-scratch baseline. Repeat with each domain held out.

* SM-6 – Forensic Report Generation: The system produces a structured incident report for at least 2 multi-camera scenarios, combining evidence into a coherent timeline. How to measure: On AI City Challenge footage (the only multi-camera dataset), run the full retrieve-synthesize-generate pipeline and validate each report against a scenario checklist (correct cameras, right chronology, evidence-grounded claims).

### **4.2 Tests** {#4.2-tests}

The test plan below provides comprehensive functional test cases for each requirement, organized by system component. Each test case specifies inputs, expected results, and the dataset or environment used for validation.

| Test ID | FR | Test Description | Input / Action | Expected Result |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| TC-1 | FR- 1a/b/c | Ingestion, frame selection, queue | Upload MP4. connect RTSP. ingest 30s | Both sources decoded. motion frames selected. published to queue. workers consume async |
| TC-2 | FR-2 | Embedding generation | Process 30s segments | Frame \+ clip embeddings generated |
| TC-3 | FR-3 | Vector storage | Insert embeddings into Qdrant | Stored with timestamp, camera ID, thumbnail |
| TC-4 | FR-4 | NL semantic search | Insert 100K+ embeddings. run NL queries | ANN \< 100 ms. Top-5 recall ≥ 85%. fused ranking |
| TC-5 | FR-5 | PFM forecasting | Run 30s/60s windows. train 2 domains. fine-tune 3rd | Top-3 ≥ 70%. EWT ≥ 15 s. FP ≤ 20%. transfer ≥ 10% |
| TC-6 | FR-6 | Agentic verification | Run 4-stage pipeline on anomaly \+ benign | Anomalies escalated. benign suppressed. FP reduced |
| TC-7 | FR-7 | RAG dossier | Trigger multi-camera synthesis | Timestamped dossier. cross-camera correlations traceable. |
| TC-8 | FR-8 | MCP server | Agent calls search, temporal, predict, alert tools | All exposed. schema-valid. real-time alerts. |
| TC-9 | FR-9 | Dashboard | Run search, view alerts, open dossiers | All views render correctly |
| TC-10 | FR-10 | Authentication | Authorized \+ unauthorized requests | Valid accepted. invalid → 401/403 |
| TC-11 | FR-11 | Retry on failure | Kill worker. stop Qdrant. inject drops | Retried until success. dead-letter on terminal; zero loss |

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# **5\. AI Tools and Prompts Used**

AI assistants were used during Phase A as auxiliary tooling — Perplexity to build the literature foundation, then Claude to turn that foundation into a connected engineering design — under continuous human review. All scientific decisions and final wording remain authored by the team.

## **5.1 Perplexity**

Used first, to build the literature base of the book: finding the foundational and recent papers behind every project component, and gathering trusted references for Chapter 2\.  
*Example prompts:*

* "find the main papers behind vision-language models for open-vocabulary video search"  
* "recent work on event anticipation and predictive forecasting in video"  
* "datasets for traffic anomaly detection with precursor annotations"

## **5.2 Claude (Anthropic)**

Used after the literature base was in place, for the engineering phase: taking the project idea and making sure every piece fits — algorithms connecting into one pipeline, datasets matching the algorithms that consume them, and diagrams reflecting the actual design.  
*Example prompts:*

* "here are my six algorithms — make sure they connect into one pipeline and the inputs/outputs line up"  
* "i'm using VIRAT, UCF-Crime, SoccerNet, DoTA, and AI City Challenge — does each one actually match the algorithm and success metric i assigned it to?"  
* "give me mermaid code for the deployment architecture: frontend, backend with 4 services, data tier, rabbitmq between them"

## **5.3 Workflow**

Perplexity came first — to discover and verify external sources that grounded the literature review and the design choices. Claude came second — to take those grounded ideas and make sure the system fits together end-to-end. Every value taken from a model response was cross-checked against the original paper before entering the book.

## 

## 

## 

## 

## **6\. References**

**Academic References (APA, alphabetical):**

1\. Bai, S., et al. (2025). Qwen2.5-VL: Enhancing vision-language model’s perception of the world at any resolution. arXiv preprint, arXiv:2502.13923.

2\. Cao, C., Lu, Y., Wang, P., & Zhang, Y. (2023). A new comprehensive benchmark for semi-supervised video anomaly detection and anticipation. Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR). arXiv:2305.13611.

3\. Cheng, Y., et al. (2026). VADER: Towards causal video anomaly understanding with relation-aware large language models. Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV). arXiv:2511.07299.

4\. Felsen, P., Lucey, P., & Ganguly, S. (2017). What will happen next? Forecasting player moves in sports videos. Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV).

5\. Fioresi, J., et al. (2025). Privacy beyond pixels: Latent anonymization for privacy-preserving video understanding. arXiv preprint, arXiv:2511.08666.

6\. Giancola, S., Amine, M., Dghaily, T., & Ghanem, B. (2018). SoccerNet: A scalable dataset for action spotting in soccer videos. Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW).

7\. Malkov, Y. A., & Yashunin, D. A. (2020). Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using hierarchical navigable small world graphs. IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 42(4), 824–836. arXiv:1603.09320.

8\. Mao, Y., et al. (2025). Multi-RAG: A multimodal retrieval-augmented generation system for adaptive video understanding. arXiv preprint, arXiv:2505.23990.

9\. Oh, S., Hoogs, A., Perera, A., et al. (2011). A large-scale benchmark dataset for event recognition in surveillance video. Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR).

10\. Papadopoulos, G., et al. (2024). GenDFIR: Advancing cyber incident timeline analysis through RAG and LLMs. MDPI Computers.

11\. Radford, A., Kim, J. W., Hallacy, C., et al. (2021). Learning transferable visual models from natural language supervision. Proceedings of the 38th International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML).

12\. Sultani, W., Chen, C., & Shah, M. (2018). Real-world anomaly detection in surveillance videos. Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR). arXiv:1801.04264.

13\. Tschannen, M., et al. (2025). SigLIP 2: Multilingual vision-language encoders with improved semantic understanding. arXiv preprint, arXiv:2502.14786.

14\. Vaswani, A., Shazeer, N., Parmar, N., et al. (2017). Attention is all you need. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 30\.

15\. Wang, Y., Li, K., Li, Y., et al. (2024). InternVideo2: Scaling foundation models for multimodal video understanding. Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV). arXiv:2403.15377.

16\. Yao, Y., Wang, X., Xu, M., et al. (2022). DOTA: Unsupervised detection of traffic anomaly in driving videos. IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence.

**Non-Academic / Technical References:**

17\. AI City Challenge. (2025). Track 2: Multi-camera traffic safety analysis. https://www.aicitychallenge.org

18\. Anthropic. (2025). Model Context Protocol specification. https://modelcontextprotocol.io

19\. Qdrant. (2025). Vector similarity search engine — Documentation. https://qdrant.tech/documentation

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# 

# **Appendices**

### **3.5.1 Algorithm 1 – ROI-Aware Spatial-Motion Sampling**  {#3.5.1-algorithm-1-–-roi-aware-spatial-motion-sampling-1}

The ingestion service holds a per-camera rolling buffer of length 2Δ. After a 2Δ warm-up, the sampler partitions the grayscale difference between f\_t and f\_{t−Δ} into a G×G grid and admits f\_t if at least k cells exceed τ ∈ \[0, 255\]. On admission, the causal clip W \= \[t − 2Δ, t\] is read from the buffer and (f\_t, W) is forwarded downstream. The causal window removes lookahead latency for real-time alerts and ensures W is fully buffered.

| Input:   f\_t, G, τ ∈ \[0, 255\], k, Buffer\_cam, Δ Output:  forwarded payload (f\_t, W) OR drop OR warmup   1:  Buffer\_cam.append(f\_t) 2:  IF Buffer\_cam.duration \< 2Δ THEN RETURN warmup 3:  diff   ← |grayscale(f\_t) − grayscale(f\_{t−Δ})| 4:  cells  ← partition(diff, G × G) 5:  active ← count(c ∈ cells : mean(c) \> τ) 6:  IF active ≥ k THEN 7:  	W ← Buffer\_cam.window(\[t − 2Δ, t\]) 8:  	forward(f\_t, W) 9:  ELSE drop(f\_t)  |
| :---- |

### **3.5.2 Algorithm 2 – Dual-Stream Embedding Generation** {#3.5.2-algorithm-2-–-dual-stream-embedding-generation-1}

Each forwarded payload is processed by two asynchronous workers. The frame-level path runs SigLIP 2 on f\_t for static scene semantics. The clip-level path runs InternVideo2\_clip (768-D) on W subsampled to its required 8 frames at 224×224 for temporal semantics. Both embeddings are L2-normalized and upserted into Qdrant with shared metadata (including frame\_id) so frame-level and clip-level results can be joined during fusion.

| Input:   (f\_t, W) from Alg. 1; camera\_id, timestamp, domain Output:  L2-normalized embeddings stored in Qdrant   1:  e\_f ← SigLIP2\_image(f\_t); e\_f ← e\_f / ‖e\_f‖₂ 2:  W\_s ← uniform\_subsample(W, 8 frames @ 224 × 224\) 3:  e\_c ← InternVideo2\_clip(W\_s); e\_c ← e\_c / ‖e\_c‖₂ 4:  meta ← {timestamp, camera\_id, domain,         	frame\_id \= hash(camera\_id, timestamp), thumb(f\_t)} 5:  Qdrant.upsert(DB\_frame, e\_f, meta) 6:  Qdrant.upsert(DB\_clip,  e\_c, meta)  Move to Appendix |
| :---- |

### **3.5.3 Dual-Model Score Fusion for Semantic Retrieval** {#3.5.3-dual-model-score-fusion-for-semantic-retrieval-1}

Queries vary by modality: scenes ("red car near entrance") vs. actions ("person running away"). A classifier C(q) outputs softmax weights (w\_f, w\_c). The query is encoded once by each text encoder (each model only operates in its own joint space). Because raw cosine scores from SigLIP 2 and InternVideo2 live on incomparable scales, fusion uses **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)** over rank positions — scale-invariant and the standard for combining heterogeneous retrievers. The constant **k₀ \= 60** is the value recommended by Cormack et al. (2009). 

| Input:   query q, K, k₀ \= 60 (RRF constant) Output:  ranked top-K list R   1:  (w\_f, w\_c) ← C(q)                	// softmax, w\_f \+ w\_c \= 1 2:  v\_f ← SigLIP2\_text(q);  v\_c ← InternVideo2\_text(q) 3:  cand\_f ← HNSW(DB\_frame, v\_f, K);  cand\_c ← HNSW(DB\_clip, v\_c, K) 4:  M ← unique frame\_ids in cand\_f ∪ cand\_c 5:  FOR each m ∈ M: 6:  	r\_f ← rank in cand\_f (∞ if absent) 7:  	r\_c ← rank in cand\_c (∞ if absent) 8:  	score(m) ← w\_f / (k₀ \+ r\_f) \+ w\_c / (k₀ \+ r\_c) 9:  RETURN top-K of sort(M, by score, desc) Move to Appendix |
| :---- |

[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image2]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image3]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image4]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image5]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image6]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image7]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image8]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image9]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image10]: <data:image/png;base64,>
